refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency compatible with safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR is already focused on a single obligation: ensuring teleoperation latency is compatible with safe remote operation. It does not combine multiple requirements or specify implementation details, thus it is atomic and solution-neutral.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the end-to-end communication latency during teleoperation under agreed network conditions and verify that the latency meets the safety criteria (e.g., p95 ≤ 150 ms).
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency compatible with safe remote operation.

candidate_dps: Implement a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation
Design a real-time data compression and prioritization mechanism to minimize transmission delays
Establish a distributed control architecture with local autonomy to reduce dependency on end-to-end communication
Incorporate predictive control algorithms to compensate for communication latency
Use a modular communication interface that can adapt to varying network conditions
selected_dp.selected_dp: Implement a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation

potential_sources: Tight integration between communication protocol and control system could cause coupling
Dependency on specific network conditions may limit flexibility
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP focuses on the communication protocol layer, which logically separates it from control algorithms and hardware, supporting independence. However, care must be taken to avoid embedding control logic within the communication stack to maintain decoupling.

assumptions_risks: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters to meet latency requirements
Operational policies do not impose additional constraints that increase latency
Predictive control or local autonomy is not required to meet latency targets
The communication protocol can be implemented without excessive complexity or cost
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Consider explicitly including adaptive mechanisms or local autonomy in the DP to handle variability in network conditions and further ensure latency requirements are met under all operational scenarios.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Module responsible for enabling remote control and communication with the vehicle, including teleoperation-specific communication protocols.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bi-directional data flow for vehicle status and control feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands and control instructions sent to the teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical and logical network interface for communication with remote operator.

module_containment_scores.notes: Very broad module; contains many unrelated functions, leading to high diffusion of the DP.
Vehicle module focuses on physical vehicle systems; communication protocol stack for teleoperation is only partially relevant, causing high diffusion.
Most specific and appropriate module; designed for teleoperation functions including communication protocols, resulting in high cohesion and low diffusion.
Safety Services focus on safety alerts and monitoring, not communication protocol implementation, leading to poor containment and high diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and appropriate boundary to contain the dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation, as it directly manages remote control communication and related protocols.
containment_risks: Potential overlap with Network Link interface responsibilities if physical network handling is outside Teleoperation.
If Teleoperation module is not well-defined or too thin, some protocol logic might diffuse into NGHE Solution or Vehicle modules.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR by specifying a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation, which is necessary to achieve the required end-to-end communication latency compatible with safe remote operation.

diffusion_risks: Potential overlap with Network Link interface responsibilities if physical network handling is outside Teleoperation.
If Teleoperation module is not well-defined or too thin, some protocol logic might diffuse into NGHE Solution or Vehicle modules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module has a high containment score (9) and low diffusion score (2), indicating it is the most appropriate and cohesive boundary for implementing the DP. Other modules show poor containment and high diffusion, supporting this allocation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible by measuring the teleoperation latency (p95 ≤ 150 ms) under agreed network conditions, which can be monitored through the Teleoperation module's interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The Teleoperation module clearly owns the implementation of the communication protocol stack, with well-defined interfaces supporting verification and operational control.

recommended_revisions: Clarify and document the responsibilities and boundaries between the Teleoperation module and the Network Link interface to avoid overlap and diffusion of protocol handling.
Ensure the Teleoperation module is sufficiently comprehensive to contain all protocol logic to prevent diffusion into broader modules like NGHE Solution or Vehicle.
Define interface contracts explicitly to manage data flow and responsibilities, reducing risks of diffusion and improving modular ownership clarity.

decision.justification: All gates (FR->DP, DP->IM, FR->IM) have passed with strong justification and verification feasibility. The Teleoperation module is well-defined with high containment and low diffusion, making it the appropriate implementation boundary. Although some minor risks of diffusion and interface overlap exist, these are addressed by recommended revisions and do not warrant further decomposition at this stage. Given the high safety criticality, maintaining a clear and cohesive module boundary is preferable to avoid complexity and potential integration issues.

revise.why: Clarify and document the responsibilities and boundaries between the Teleoperation module and Network Link interface to avoid overlap and diffusion of protocol handling, and ensure the module is comprehensive enough to contain all protocol logic.

assumptions: The Teleoperation module can fully contain the communication protocol stack logic.
Network conditions and latency measurements are reliable and representative for verification.
Interface contracts can be clearly defined and enforced.
open_risks: Potential overlap with Network Link interface responsibilities if physical network handling is outside Teleoperation.
Risk of protocol logic diffusion into NGHE Solution or Vehicle modules if Teleoperation module is too thin.
evidence_needed: Documentation of interface contracts between Teleoperation and Network Link modules.
Verification data showing teleoperation latency meets p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.
Containment and diffusion metrics supporting module boundary decisions.
